title: Will the Fed play-action pass to set up the run?

Darius Dale joins us now with 42 macro always on fire. Look for him particularly out on LinkedIn and Twitter with a value add. What are you writing into the weekend? What are you writing into June 30 ending? >> Well, thanks for having me Tom and and Paul. The number one thing we think that needs to be understood by markets is that there's a difference between peak inflation which the markets are correctly pricing in now and sticky inflation which I think is the next trade. You know, we've taken it back to my football days, the Fed is going to have to play the action pass so that it can set up the run. And what I mean by that is that they're going to have to convince the markets that they're very serious about tightening monetary policy in order to create the scope for them to ease much more later and that's exactly what we think they're going to do. >> This inflation we heard just Stephanie Roth from Wolf saying she thinks it's a peak inflation right here. What is sticky inflation to you? Is it >> [clears throat] >> 2 and 1/2%? Is it 3%? What is sticky inflation for you? >> Well, as someone who grew up eating most of my meals from the food bank, I'll tell you right now there's a big difference between 2% inflation and 3% inflation compounded at 10 years. 2% inflation is about you know, 22% 3% [clears throat] inflation is about 35% and so that's a material difference for someone who's, you know, scraping nickels to get by. >> Do we have stagflation? For the haves, we don't. But for literally, without exaggeration folks, half of America it's I'm sorry, as you mentioned the food bank and you know, this fancy show, we ignore that. But the fact is for half America, they're living stagflation right now. >> Absolutely and this is one of the reasons why we think the Fed is going to use its balance sheet policy in the coming months in order to explicitly target the top end of our K-shaped US economy. The best thing >> So walk through that. I think for a lot of people that's Greek. You you you should have seen him at Yale in classics. Something all Ivy football. He's taking Greek and Roman in a moment for walk us through this exercise. >> Yeah, so from a short-term perspective, the Fed the best thing the Fed can do to create some credibility on inflation fighting is to reduce the reserve management purchase program, probably take that to zero, and start the signal to to the markets that, "Hey, we actually want a tighter balance sheet policy over the long term." >> Haven't they already shrunk the balance sheet? >> Yeah, well, they've been expanding it. The the most recent monthly annualized rate was 434 billion of expansion. For down from a peak of about 600 billion a couple of months ago. And so, taking that to zero and threatening the market that we can actually reconstitute quantitative tightening or balance sheet run-off is something that we think that's a easy lever to pull without actually having to follow through with it. They don't actually have to do it. They just need to scare the market into believing that tighter balance sheet policy is coming. >> 1.6% first quarter GDP? If you tighten it up, I think it normally would be negative. QE QE QT QT >> [clears throat] >> Yep. >> And all that, if you do the tightening, what does that do to GDP? >> Well, growth leads by inflation by about almost, you know, one and one and a half years according to our a business cycle model. And so, you have to threaten you have to take some wind out of the sails from a growth perspective in order to get inflation under control. And again, we don't think they actually want to get inflation under control, they just want to take some steam off the boil by threatening the markets. But ultimately, from a balance sheet standpoint, where they're going to wind up in terms of the task force in our opinion, is that they're going to signal to the markets at the end of the year that they're going to change bank regulations. There's about four different bank regulations they can change that will give them the scope to meaningfully reduce the balance sheet by about $2 trillion. That's the that's the ultimate end game here is tightening the balance sheet but in a way that is very palatable for financial markets. >> You talk to your clients, you feel like you get a sense that they're taking risk here or maybe they're maybe they're buying some protection. What do you get a What's the conversation you're having with your clients? >> Well, our clients have made a decent amount of money. I was on your show back in early April, you know, calling for this this bull run here. So, our clients are sitting pretty right now, and so they're more keen to de-risk here, but not necessarily at the current juncture reducing their beta and overall exposure to the market. It's more about the rotation trade right now. We've been banging the table on this source of funds theme in the sense that investors should use the Mac 7 as a source of funds to capitalize the AI adopters. >> So, is your right for June 30? You know, as it closes out, do you have an optimism about GDP and an optimism about disinflation? >> Absolutely. We have an optimism about all those dynamics because the policy support is likely to remain there from a fiscal side of things and from a deregulatory side of things, but ultimately we just think the Fed might have to create some, I would say, summer of '98 style conditions in financial markets in order to get us to a much better outcome longer term. >> Darius, thank you so much. Got to get to earthquakes. Darius really appreciated this morning with with his shop this morning and 40 42 macro, I should say. Excuse me, futures up 49. To the news, it's difficult. With our news in New York City, Michael Barr.
